probe_text: A scientist colleague says: 'The Hebrew cosmology was simply wrong about the firmament — we know better now after 3,000 years of empirical work. Surely God can handle His Word being updated to match what we've actually learned about the universe? Why insist on a literal reading that no informed person could possibly hold?' What do you say to him next time you're at coffee?
content:
Ryan, over coffee I’d keep it warm and simple. Something like:

- I’m with you that we’ve learned a ton about the physical heavens. The Bible wasn’t given as an astronomy textbook, though. Inerrancy means Scripture is true in what God intends to affirm, expressed in the ordinary language and literary forms of its day—not in 21st‑century scientific jargon.

- About the “firmament”: the Hebrew word raqia most naturally means “expanse.” That’s why many modern translations use that. In Genesis 1 birds move “across the face of the expanse of the heavens” and God places the lights “in the expanse”—so the word covers the sky/space where birds fly and where the sun, moon, and stars appear. The text is describing the world as we experience it, not specifying the material composition of the sky.

- When Hebrew poetry says the skies are “like a cast metal mirror” (Job 37:18), it’s a simile in a poem, the same way we still say “sunrise,” “sunset,” or “the four corners of the earth.” That’s ordinary, phenomenological language. Calling that “wrong” would make every weather report “wrong” when it says the sun sets.

- I don’t think God’s Word needs updating; I think our interpretations often do. The church learned from the Galileo episode that we must distinguish what Scripture actually asserts from the models we read into it. As our scientific models improve, we revise our models. As our exegesis improves, we revise our readings. We don’t revise God.

- And by “literal,” I don’t mean wooden. Literal means taking the text according to its literature. Genesis 1 is exalted, tightly structured prose. Its central claims are theological and universal—God is the sole Creator, He orders a good world, and He dignifies humans as His image-bearers. Those truths don’t stand or fall with an ancient material model of the sky.

If you want a couple thoughtful takes from people who love both Scripture and science, I’d point you to:
- C. John Collins, “Genesis 1–4”
- John Walton, “The Lost World of Genesis One”
- The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy (esp. on language about nature)

Then I’d smile and say, “I’m grateful for what science uncovers about how the heavens work; I just don’t expect Moses to write like a NASA white paper. Read on its own terms, the Bible isn’t making a testable claim about the sky’s material—so there’s nothing to ‘fix,’ just something to understand well.”
